authoritative_sources.title: AMS Air Features
authoritative_sources.source_class: Transportation, customs, or safety regulator guidance
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: CBP is the U.S. customs authority and its Air AMS guidance explains advance electronic air cargo information expectations used to judge whether customs documentation is ready for compliant movement.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: customs documentation prerequisite review, inbound air cargo data handoff, customs blocker escalation
used_for: AMS and air cargo information boundary

authoritative_sources.title: 19 CFR 122.48a - Electronic information for air cargo required in advance of arrival
authoritative_sources.source_class: Transportation, customs, or safety regulator guidance
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: This CBP regulation directly defines advance electronic air cargo information requirements for inbound air cargo, making it authoritative for customs-data prerequisites and escalation when information is missing.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: advance cargo data validation, customs data blocker review, adjacent customs/compliance routing
used_for: ACAS or AMS data prerequisite handling

authoritative_sources.title: 19 CFR Part 122 - Air Commerce Regulations
authoritative_sources.source_class: Transportation, customs, or safety regulator guidance
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: CBP air commerce regulations define manifest, arrival, clearance, cargo, and reporting duties for aircraft and air cargo, constraining international movement and customs handoff claims.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: manifest and air commerce dependency review, international cargo movement blocker handling, customs-owner routing

authoritative_sources.title: 49 CFR Part 175 - Carriage by Aircraft
authoritative_sources.source_class: Transportation, customs, or safety regulator guidance
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: PHMSA hazardous-materials-by-air rules define aircraft-operator acceptance, shipping-paper, inspection, pilot-in-command notification, loading, stowage, discrepancy, and retention requirements for dangerous goods.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: dangerous-goods documentation review, NOTOC discrepancy escalation, safety-critical movement blocker handling

authoritative_sources.title: 49 CFR Part 1548 - Indirect Air Carrier Security
authoritative_sources.source_class: Transportation, customs, or safety regulator guidance
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: TSA indirect air carrier security rules govern regulated air cargo security-program obligations for indirect air carriers, making screening and secure-chain handling an adjacent authority rather than a coordination decision.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: security-program boundary review, cargo security handoff, regulated-owner escalation

authoritative_sources.title: 49 CFR Part 1549 - Certified Cargo Screening Program
authoritative_sources.source_class: Chain-of-custody, dispatch, or movement workflow references
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: TSA certified cargo screening rules define screening facility duties and cargo search refusal requirements, making security chain-of-custody and screening evidence a regulated handoff prerequisite.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: screening evidence review, secure-chain handoff control, cargo search refusal escalation

authoritative_sources.title: e-AWB
authoritative_sources.source_class: Chain-of-custody, dispatch, or movement workflow references
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: IATA is a recognized air transport standards body and e-AWB is its public reference for electronic air waybill adoption, shipment contract data, and carrier-forwarder documentation handoffs.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: booking and air waybill prerequisite review, carrier-forwarder handoff packaging, documentation gap escalation

authoritative_sources.title: ONE Record
authoritative_sources.source_class: Chain-of-custody, dispatch, or movement workflow references
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: IATA ONE Record is the recognized air cargo data-sharing standard for connected shipment data across airlines, forwarders, ground handlers, customs, and service providers.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: cross-party data retrieval, source-of-truth mapping, cargo status and handoff traceability

authoritative_sources.title: Cargo Handling Manual
authoritative_sources.source_class: Equipment, route, or service-level operating standards
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: IATA cargo handling guidance is a recognized operating reference for cargo acceptance, terminal handling, build-up, ramp transfer, breakdown, delivery, and station handoff terminology.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: warehouse and station handoff packaging, build-up or breakdown dependency tracking, ramp transfer and delivery closure evidence
used_for: cargo acceptance and terminal handling stages

authoritative_sources.title: Unit Load Devices
authoritative_sources.source_class: Equipment, route, or service-level operating standards
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: IATA ULD references are recognized public air cargo operating references for pallets, containers, build-up, serviceability, aircraft loading interface, and custody handoffs.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: ULD build-up and serviceability review, load and ramp transfer dependency tracking, equipment blocker escalation

authoritative_sources.title: Cargo iQ Quality Management
authoritative_sources.source_class: Equipment, route, or service-level operating standards
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: Cargo iQ is a recognized air cargo quality-management body whose shipment milestone framework supports service-level, route, schedule, and exception handling across air cargo parties.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: milestone and service-level dependency tracking, route exception handling, closure confirmation and customer-impact packaging

workflow_stages.name: Dependency intake and authority classification
workflow_stages.description: Normalize the requested air cargo outcome, shipment identifiers, booking, AWB references, origin, transfer station, destination, route, flight, service level, cargo type, owner context, and intended action, then classify the work as in-scope, out-of-scope, or blocked before routing.
source_classes: Transportation, customs, or safety regulator guidance
Chain-of-custody, dispatch, or movement workflow references

workflow_stages.name: Prerequisite and system-of-record validation
workflow_stages.description: Validate TMS, WMS, cargo booking, cargo management, customs, screening, DG, ULD, dispatch-reference, telematics, maintenance, customer operations, and service records before handoff summaries, blocker logs, or closure confirmations are produced.
source_classes: Chain-of-custody, dispatch, or movement workflow references
Equipment, route, or service-level operating standards

workflow_stages.name: Handoff package assembly
workflow_stages.description: Prepare a package that names prior owner, next owner, required artifacts, handoff state, blocker state, source systems, custody posture, and closure criteria for shipper, forwarder, carrier, warehouse, ramp, customs, security, DG, maintenance, or customer operations handoffs.
source_classes: Chain-of-custody, dispatch, or movement workflow references
Equipment, route, or service-level operating standards

workflow_stages.name: Movement, custody, and route exception tracking
workflow_stages.description: Track booking, RCS, MAN, DEP, ARR, RCF, NFD, DLV, POD, ULD, load, transfer, screening, customs, DG, offload, delay, diversion, route, and service-level dependencies while preserving conflicting records as blockers.
source_classes: Transportation, customs, or safety regulator guidance
Chain-of-custody, dispatch, or movement workflow references
Equipment, route, or service-level operating standards

workflow_stages.name: Adjacent authority routing
workflow_stages.description: Route customs release, AMS or ACAS sufficiency, cargo screening, secure-chain certification, dangerous-goods acceptance, NOTOC sufficiency, dispatch or flight operations decisions, maintenance release, safety signoff, financial approval, and legal interpretation to the correct adjacent owner.
source_classes: Transportation, customs, or safety regulator guidance

workflow_stages.name: Closure confirmation and audit-ready transfer
workflow_stages.description: Confirm closure only when required artifacts, source-system evidence, destination-owner acknowledgement, blocker resolution, residual risk posture, and authority boundaries are documented; otherwise keep the handoff blocked or escalated.
source_classes: Chain-of-custody, dispatch, or movement workflow references
Equipment, route, or service-level operating standards

artifacts: shipment, booking, MAWB, HAWB, e-AWB, FWB, FHL, cargo manifest, and delivery order records
origin, transfer, destination, route, flight, truck, cutoff, capacity, embargo, offload, delay, diversion, recovery, and service-level records
warehouse acceptance, piece count, weight, dimensions, ULD, pallet or container build-up, load plan, ramp transfer, arrival, breakdown, delivery, POD, and chain-of-custody scan records
AMS, ACAS, customs entry, consignee, broker, import, export, and agency documentation or status references
cargo screening, known-shipper, secure-chain, access-control, seal, custody, and security-program status records supplied by authorized owners
dangerous goods declaration, shipping paper, NOTOC, lithium battery, special-handling, temperature-control, live animal, perishable, pharmaceutical, and other special cargo records
equipment status, ULD serviceability, GSE status, telematics, fleet, maintenance status, service logs, and station handoff notes
customer commitment, exception ticket, owner acknowledgement, blocker log, routing note, escalation note, and closure confirmation records
systems_of_record: customer operations, CRM, and service-desk systems
station handoff log and cargo control-tower tracker

allowed: classify air cargo coordination requests as in-scope, out-of-scope, or blocked on missing prerequisites
request or validate tenant facts, authoritative records, source freshness, and retrieval hooks required to continue safely
prepare evidence-backed handoff summaries, dependency trackers, blocker notes, route-impact summaries, and closure confirmations
identify prior owner, next owner, required artifact set, blocker state, and destination-owner acknowledgement requirements
route to adjacent dispatch, customs, security, dangerous-goods, maintenance, warehouse, ramp, airline operations, airport operations, or customer operations owners when ownership shifts
escalate: shipment movement depends on customs, cargo security, dangerous-goods, dispatch, safety, maintenance, financial, legal, or policy approval
custody evidence, AWB records, screening status, customs data, DG paperwork, ULD records, or destination acknowledgement are missing or contradictory
route disruption, flight cancellation, offload, diversion, embargo, or schedule change may alter customs, screening, DG, service-level, or customer commitment posture
multiple teams claim the same handoff step or no owner can be uniquely identified
tenant policy and public source baseline conflict materially on closure, custody, screening, customs, DG, or route handling
refuse: approve or certify customs release, AMS or ACAS sufficiency, cargo screening, known-shipper status, DG acceptance, NOTOC sufficiency, dispatch release, safety signoff, maintenance release, financial approval, or legal interpretation
book, tender, load, screen, dispatch, move, release, deliver, or update cargo systems as the execution owner
mark shipment, ULD, ramp transfer, customs handoff, secure-chain step, or delivery complete without required records and destination-owner acknowledgement
synthesize a confident answer when required tenant facts, records, source context, or authority maps are missing and would change the outcome

domain_failure_modes: Cargo moves or is treated as ready without complete AWB, manifest, screening, customs, DG, ULD, or custody evidence.
A chain-of-custody break is hidden by generic status language instead of being escalated with missing scans and owner acknowledgements.
The coordination lane implies customs release, cargo security certification, DG acceptance, dispatch, maintenance, or safety authority.
Route, flight, offload, diversion, embargo, or schedule disruption changes compliance posture but the handoff proceeds as routine.
Multiple teams or systems claim the same handoff step, causing stale ownership or dropped work.
Tenant-specific codes are accepted without mapping them to public air cargo artifact and milestone expectations.
Closure is confirmed without POD, destination owner acceptance, source-system evidence, or residual blocker state.
Public source baseline and tenant SOP conflict materially, but the lane invents a synthesis instead of escalating.

scenario_family_coverage.scenario_family: Route or schedule disruption with compliance impact
Request that implies customs, dispatch, or safety authority
Work is handed off without required context or artifacts
Multiple teams appear to own the same step
The lane is asked to absorb execution work instead of coordinating it
Ownership is ambiguous across multiple teams
A handoff is attempted without the required context

unresolved_ambiguities.topic: Jurisdiction-specific customs, security, and dangerous-goods rules outside the public U.S. and IATA baseline
unresolved_ambiguities.handling: The validated public baseline is sufficient for package scope; runtime must require tenant jurisdiction, carrier, station, broker, security-program, and DG manual context before acting on affected handoffs.

unresolved_ambiguities.topic: Tenant-specific cargo status codes and source-system priority order
unresolved_ambiguities.handling: The spec requires tenant terminology maps and system-of-record precedence at runtime; scenarios verify that unknown local codes are normalized or escalated rather than guessed.
